summary: Kenneth Suna demonstrates rolling covered calls and evaluates whether to sell cash-secured puts on HIMS stock. He decides against rolling (to avoid eating into the $25 premium) and against selling a cash-secured put (gap risk too high relative to $188 premium), instead planning to repurchase shares Monday and sell a new covered call at a higher strike.
takeaways: Rolling a covered call means buying back the contract and selling a new one for a later week—use this when you want to keep shares and have a low cost basis with big tax consequences
A cash-secured put requires capital equal to 100 shares × strike price (e.g., $5,700 for a $57 strike on HIMS at $63)
Put premium is typically lower than call premium on the same stock at similar strikes
Gap risk at market open (stock could open well below strike over a weekend) can erase multiple weeks of premium—skip the trade if the risk-to-reward doesn't justify it
When assignment is likely on a covered call, compare the net profit (strike gain + premium) versus opportunity cost if stock rallies past strike
For a $50k portfolio targeting $1,250/mo premium, prioritize high-probability near-the-money strikes over long-shot out-of-the-money strikes with low premium

strategy_candidates.change: Skip cash-secured puts with gap risk unless premium is ≥0.5% of required capital per week; on HIMS example, $188 on $5,700 cash = 3.3% weekly—too low given weekend risk
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth explicitly rejects the $61 put on HIMS despite $188 premium because 'gap risk' at market open could force him to buy at $61 when stock opens at $51, turning the trade into a loss. He states: 'I would rather miss out on the hundred bucks' rather than take that risk. This suggests a rule: premium must justify the capital lock and event risk.

title: Part 3.5: How to Roll Covered Calls and Sell Cash Secured Puts
